DISCOVERY 301, JAN-27-2026 RE: MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO PLAINTIFFS REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS SET ONE AND REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS MEMORANDUM OF POINTS AND AUTHORITIES IN SUPPORT THEREOF. NO APPEARANCE WAS MADE BY ANY PARTY, COUNSEL, OR INTERESTED PERSON. THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING AS FOLLOWS: PLAINTIFFS' (CHL MEDICAL PARTNERS III SIDE FUND L.P. AND CHL MEDICAL PARTNERS III, L.P. (COLLECTIVELY CHL)) MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO THEIR REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS FROM LEERINK REVELATION HEALTH CARE FUND I, L.P., IS DENIED. SEE MINI MINUTES FOR COMPLETE RULING. JUDGE: CHRISTINE VAN AKEN. CLERK: ROBERT WOODS. NOT REPORTED. (301/CVA) ***AFTER HEARING AN ORDER IS EXECUTED.

calendar.matters: Application To File Documents Under Seal; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities

Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production Of Documents Set One And Request For Sanctions Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Jan-27-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PLAINTIFF CHL MEDICAL PARTNERS III SIDE FUND, L.P.'s, A DELAWARE LIMITED PARTNERSHIP, CHL MEDICAL PARTNERS III, L.P., A DELAWARE Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production Of Documents Set One And Request For Sanctions Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiffs' (CHL Medical Partners III Side Fund L.P. and CHL Medical Partners III, L.P. (collectively CHL)) motion to compel further responses to their requests for production of documents from Leerink Revelation Health Care Fund I, L.P., is denied.

CHL and Leerink entered into contracts whose alleged breach is the subject of this case. In the course of negotiating those contracts, CHL and Leerink were represented by different lawyers from a single large law firm. Although there was some discussion of a conflict waiver, apparently no waivers were ever signed. Leerink's principal attests that he never signed a conflict waiver, and further that he expected the attorney-client privilege protected his communications. (Boggs Decl., paras. 15-16.) Neither party presents evidence whether the law firm took steps to wall off the respective lawyers for each party.

Evidence Code, section 962, provides: "Where two or more clients have retained or consulted a lawyer upon a matter of common interest, none of them ... may claim a privilege under this article as to a communication made in the course of that relationship when such communication is offered in a civil proceeding between one of such clients (or his successor in interest) and another of such clients (or his successor in interest)."

The formation of an attorney-client relationship is a matter of agreement or contract, express or implied. (Shen v. Miller (2012) 212 Cal.App.4th 48, 56-57.) Thus, the court tries to discern from the facts presented whether the parties intended to be represented not just by the same law firm but by the same lawyer, such that they would have no privilege against each other. Here, it appears they intended to have separate lawyers. They were represented by different lawyers of the large firm, and there is no indication that CHL communicated separately with Leerink's lawyer or vice versa. The negotiation of a contract between counterparties is not a situation where parties are typically represented by the same counsel without a conflict waiver, and although the parties have a common interest in achieving a contract that benefits them both, their interests along the way are often adverse.

This result is in some tension with Evidence Code, section 954, which extends the privilege between attorney and client not only to the lawyer retained by the client but to all members of the lawyer's firm. The court interprets the joint-client exception to the privilege in Evidence Code, section 962, narrowly in light of the important purposes the privilege serves to encourage free communication between lawyer and client. (Sky Valley Ltd. Partnership v. ATX Sky Valley, Ltd. (N.D. Cal. 1993) 150 F.R.D. 648, 653.) The question of whether a joint client relationship was formed is a complex multifactor inquiry. Here, other than the fact of the single law firm, there is nothing suggesting that the parties intended to form a relationship that would waive their privilege as to each other.

Both sides seek sanctions. The court denies sanctions, concluding that both sides' positions had substantial justification.

A proposed order has been submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/CVA)
